1 and 5: Use the handshapes for the numbers 1 and 5 to bring to life a poetic rendering of the changing image of telephone lines along the side of the road.
19: Note that the palm faces the body for the numbers 15 and is facing away from the body for the numbers 69, and then for the numbers 1115, and then for the numbers 1619 it is once again facing away from the body.
7: Draw a number 7 in the air with your finger, starting from the top and moving downwards
8: Draw a number 8 in the air with your finger, starting from the top and moving downwards
activities: Make a circular motion above head with dominant hand.
afternoon: shows the horizon (represented by the horizontal arm) and the sun midway between the zenith and the horizon (represented by the other hand)
agent-sign: Sign AGENT-sign with a sweeping motion from the head down to the waist.
air blowing: Bend elbow and hold fist up, then blow air through fingers as if from a fan.
alerting devices: Wave hands in front of body to show flashing lights, then bring hands back to rest position.
alike: move the dominant hand with palm facing down and fingers together, touching the chin
all: hold both hands out in front of you, palms up, and move them up
all-rightokay: a fist with thumb and pinky extended, palm facing downwards
allergy: Hold up an imaginary object or person in front of you, then move it towards you and apart.
allergy medication: hold an imaginary pill in the dominant hand, touch nose with non-dominant hand
along: use a flat hand with palm facing towards you to draw a straight line on an imaginary surface
always: Move your dominant hand in a circular motion, palm facing downwards, indicating something is always the case
american: extend right arm out to the side, with palm up, and move it slightly towards oneself
anger: clench the fist with your dominant hand and tense your arm
apollogize: make an 'X' shape with both hands in front of your chest, then bring them down
approach: extend one arm forward from shoulder level, palm facing downwards
argue: clench one fist, then the other, as if punching
arrive: move the sign ARRIVE to a particular place in the signing space, decided by you
arrive-there: moves from a position in front of the body to the right side of the signing space, which is where England was placed
arrive-there early: Make an arrival gesture by walking or running into your destination, and then point to it
arrive-there-right: move your dominant hand in a downward motion towards the ground while keeping your non-dominant hand at the side of your body
art: use a 'C' handshape and move it in an arc from side to side
as-if: make a flat shape with both hands and move them as if you're performing the action described
ashamed: hold your hands down by your sides and hang your head
ask-him: The hand moves toward a place in the signing space where the person represented by HIM is placed.
associate: hold one's right arm straight out to the side, with palm down, and move it slightly
awesome: place one finger on top of another, forming a 'V' shape, and then swing it out to the side
awful: Shake head from side to side, palm facing forward.
bare: show palm facing up, fingers splayed, but not clenched
baseball game: sign 'baseball game' by making a throwing motion with your dominant hand, as if you are pitching the ball
bashful: lower head and cover face with hands, palms in
beat: clench fist and hit your other hand as if punching
bell: curl all four fingers inward, leaving thumb extended and moving it from side to side
bend: bend elbow so that forearm is parallel to ground, palm up
big: extend one arm forward from shoulder level, palm facing upwards
billiard: Mimic striking a ball on the table (using index finger as cue stick), then extend arm as if aiming for a shot
blame: point with one finger while shaking head
bleed: press the tips of your thumbs together and move them in a circular motion as if applying pressure to a wound
boat: the 3 handshape that represents a motorized land or water vehicle (car, bus, truck, van, or boat)
book: flat 'O' handshape and fingers spread, palm facing down
borrow: point with one finger while making a 'taking' motion with the other hand
bowling: extend both arms forward from shoulder level, palms up, then swing them in an arc
box: form a 'B' shape with your hands, with fingers together and thumbs apart
breathe: bend elbow so that forearm is parallel to ground, palm down, then extend arm and fist clenched
bring: extend both arms forward from shoulder level, palms up, then bring them in towards body
britain: make a curved 'V' shape with one arm and move it slightly as if waving
brochure: place both hands flat together in front of you, with fingers spread and thumbs apart
brotherhood: clasp both hands together in front of body, with fingers interlocked
bus: hold one hand out, palm up, and move it back and forth as if pushing a bus
busy: makes a busy motion with hands, as if typing or working on something
but: place the dominant hand on top of the other, forming an upside-down 'T' shape
call-out: move in the direction of where the sign WATCH-go-past ended in the previous sentence
captioning: Draw a TV screen with one hand, then move your other hand as if typing on an invisible keyboard.
car: make a flat 'C' shape with one hand, thumb on top of other fingers
carry: hold one arm straight out to the side at shoulder height, palm down
certain: point with one finger while making a nodding motion with your head
challenge: clench fist and make a 'throwing' motion as if tossing something
cheek: The index finger is extended towards the cheek to indicate the location of an action.
chicago: use the 'big city' sign by forming an imaginary skyscraper with both hands, with your thumbs on top of your fingers.
child: Pat an imaginary child's head two or three times with a flat hand.
clash: interlock fingers of both hands and move them back and forth
class: hold one's hands together, palms facing each other, and move them apart
clean: rub one hand over the other, palm to palm, in a smooth motion
clench: fist clenched with arm tense and elbow bent
climb: curl all four fingers inward, leaving thumb extended, then swing it out to the side
cloudy: move both hands up and down as if swishing through clouds
clubs: hold one hand flat in front of you, palm up, with other hand on top and fingers spread
cold: curl fingers into a fist and rub palms together
collide: move both arms forward from shoulder level, palms facing each other, then bring them in towards body
come: extend both arms forward from shoulder level, palms up, then swing them in an arc
compete: hold one arm straight out to the side at shoulder height, palm down, and make a 'victory' motion with other hand
completion: move dominant hand back and forth as if finishing something
comprehend: point with one finger while making a nodding motion with your head
computer: make a curved shape with both hands and move them in an arc, palm facing upwards
conflict: hold both arms out to the sides at shoulder height, palms down, then bring them together as if clashing
confront: Move hands apart with palms facing each other and then bring them together in a clapping motion.
contact: Touch fingertips of one hand to the fingertips of the opposite hand.
cool: Wave dominant hand back and forth, palm down.
copy: Mimic action being described, e.g. mimicking writing with index finger.
correspond: Move hands apart with palms facing each other and then bring them together in a clapping motion.
cough: Simulate coughing by bending elbow and placing hand over mouth.
counting: Hold up number of fingers to indicate quantity.
countries 57: hold one hand out, palm facing forward, to represent the number of countries, then sign COUNTRIES
countries involved: hold one's hands together, palms facing each other, and move them apart
country travel-around: move your hands in a circular motion while keeping them near waist level
courteous: Bowing or tipping head slightly, palm up.
cousin: Touch index finger of one hand to the opposite elbow joint.
critical: Point with index finger, tucking thumb under.
criticize: Flick wrist in a downward motion while pointing with index finger.
cross: Make an 'X' shape with arms at shoulder height.
crucial: Point with index finger and tap opposite elbow joint.
culture: Move hands apart and then bring them together, palm to palm.
current: Hold arm straight out in front of you, palm down, and move it up and down.
cut: Simulate cutting motion with dominant hand, e.g. slicing through air.
cycling: Mimic pedaling a bicycle by moving dominant legs, as if pushing pedals.
day: move right hand up, then outwards, away from the body
deaf: Point to ear and shake head.
deaf pride: Deaf people live in a world that is largely made for those people who can hear and speak, but they are proud of their place in society, their history and culture.
decline: Move hands apart and then bring them together, palm to palm, while leaning away from the person or object.
degree: Hold arm straight out in front of you, palm down, and move it up and down.
dentist: Mimic holding a toothbrush by moving dominant arm in a circular motion.
despondent: Hang head low and let arms drop.
didn't-know: the sign KNOW is turned away from the body
different+: When the sign DIFFERENT is repeated, its meaning is similar to VARIOUS. When signing DIFFERENT for the second time, both hands move over to the side slightly.
different+ language understand: extend right arm out to the side, with palm up, and move it slightly towards oneself, while signing LANGUAGE
differentbut: with both hands, fingers spread apart and then closed
dismal: Make a downward motion with hand, palm down.
dissection: Cutting motion with dominant hand, e.g. slicing through air.
doctor: Mimic holding a stethoscope by moving dominant arm across chest.
doctor come soon: Point to a doctor's figure or draw an imaginary picture in the air with your finger, followed by a gesture of coming quickly, such as mimicking running
don't-like: Shake head 'no' and make a negative face
downcast: Hang head low and let arms drop.
dreary: Make a downward motion with hand, palm down.
drive: Mimic driving a car by moving hands in a circular motion.
dull: Rotate dominant hand in a clockwise or counterclockwise motion.
e-mail: Hold a flat hand up with fingers together, then draw an imaginary line across the chest as if sending a message.
each-other: move both arms in a circular motion, as if embracing
easy: the dominant hand makes a smooth, flowing motion in front of the body
empty: Hold open palm of one hand and then close it.
end: Point to end of object or action.
enter: Mimic entering by moving through an imaginary door.
enthusiastic: Bounce up and down on toes, arms extended.
every: Rub the knuckles of the dominant 'A' handshape down the knuckles of the non-dominant 'A' handshape twice.
exchange: Hold two objects in each hand and trade them with the other person.
f-e-v-e-r: fingerspell the word FEVER by spelling out each letter with your dominant hand
fascinating: the non-dominant hand makes a circular motion around an object or in front of the body
fast: the dominant hand moves quickly forward and backward in front of the body
favorite: Touch the chest or the heart with the index finger, and move it down
favorite sport: Finger-spelling the word F-A-V-O-R-I-T-E S-P-O-R-T, or use a sign that represents favorite or preference
fax machine: Mimic typing on an invisible keyboard with one hand, then point to a receiver or phone.
feel: The hands are cupped in front of the chest, with fingers together and palms facing outwards.
fellowship: extend right arm out to the side, with palm up, and move it slightly towards oneself
fev-e-r have: Mimic taking temperature with an imaginary thermometer, followed by holding up two fingers
finally: It typically follows the action to which it is referring: TEACHER SHOW-UP, FINALLY! The sign FINALLY is often made with the signer mouthing the expression 'Pah!'
fine: Smile and nod head, then hold hands together in a fist (representing being fine or okay)
fingerspell: Use your dominant hand to spell out words by hand
finish: Move dominant hand downwards, palm facing forward, as if finishing something.
first: extend your index finger on one hand and move it forward, keeping other fingers closed
five: Extend five fingers on one hand, palm facing forward.
flat: Hold one hand out, palm facing upwards, with fingers together
flat surfaces: Hold one hand out, palm facing upwards, with fingers together, then touch the surface in front of you
flock-to-games: move both arms in a circular motion, as if swimming or gathering
fly: moves toward the outside of the signing space
fly-to-right: move toward the outside of the signing space
following: Use two hands, palms together, and slide them apart, then back together.
football: Extend dominant hand out, palm facing forward, with fingers spread apart.
football watch: Mimic throwing a ball or running with an open hand.
for-for: make an 'F' shape with your thumb and index finger on both hands and wiggle them
four-weeks: Hold up four fingers on one hand and move them towards your other hand to sign the length of time
funny: make a comment about something being funny by wiggling your fingers on your dominant hand
game: Make a circular motion with dominant hand, as if throwing a ball.
get-on: The hand does not move forward
get-well quick: make a grasping motion as if holding something, then point to self and move hand quickly in a circular motion
give: Mimic giving something to someone else, often done with an open palm and arm extended
go-to: Make a walking motion with dominant foot, as if taking steps.
golf: Mimic swinging a golf club by moving dominant arm in a wide arc.
good: smile and give a thumbs up with right hand
handbrake: a handshape where fingers are bent down and thumb up
handsome: the non-dominant hand is held high and parallel to the face, with the dominant hand on the hip
have: make a grasping motion with your hand as if holding something
have-to: Hold up one hand, palm facing outwards, and move it from side to side with a slight bending motion at the wrist, then tap your index finger on your chest.
he: point to the left because that is where BROTHER was placed in the signing space
hearing: Tap your ear or make a listening gesture with your hand
hello: Wave your hand towards the person you are greeting
hello d-e-n-i-s-e: Greet the person with a friendly wave or handshake, followed by signing their name (D-E-N-I-S-E)
help: begins in a neutral position and then moves toward the signer
here: Point to yourself or touch your own body
hers: open hand with the palm facing outward
his: open hand with the palm facing outward
hockey: Mimic hitting a puck with a stick by moving dominant arm in a curved motion.
hockey play: Mimic hitting a puck with a stick by moving dominant arm in a curved motion.
horseback riding: Mimic riding a horse, using dominant arm to steer an imaginary horse
hour: move right hand in a circular motion, starting from the ear and moving towards the shoulder
how-manyhow-much: move both hands up and down, with fingers spread apart
hundred: made with a C handshape, which is the Latin letter for hundred
hungry: A sign made by holding the stomach area with both hands, indicating hunger
hurt: Touch your chest area to indicate 'you', then hold up your other hand, palm facing upwards, and wiggle your fingers as if feeling something.
important: the non-dominant hand is held high and parallel to the face, with the dominant hand on the hip
in-a-while: indicates an event or action will happen at some unspecified point in the near future
in-nine-weeks: use the sign for NEXT-WEEK, but with nine fingers extended on the right hand
in-one-week: use the sign for NEXT-WEEK, but with a single finger extended on the right hand
in-two-weeks: use the sign for NEXT-WEEK, but with two fingers extended on the right hand
interact: hold one's hands together, palms facing each other, and move them apart
interactive: move both arms in a circular motion, as if engaging in conversation
internet: Draw a picture of a globe in the air, then move hands as if surfing on the internet.
involve: Mimic involvement by moving both arms up and outward, as if embracing something
involvedinclude: hold one's hands together, palms facing each other, and move them apart
its: open hand with the palm facing outward
join: Extend dominant thumb of both hands outwards to meet together at the end, palms facing upwards.
kind: Type (mimic typing on an invisible keyboard)
kiss: Gently touch tips of index fingers and thumbs of both hands together in a light pressing motion.
know: The fingertips touching the forehead once
large: Move your dominant hand in a circle with fingers extended and thumb up
large amount: Hold both hands out, palms facing upwards, with fingers spread apart
largebig: the dominant hand moves outward from the body, with palms facing outwards
last-week: use the sign for WEEK and add a backward motion with the right hand
latenot-yet: a flat hand with fingers together, moving in a 'no' motion
law: Form an 'L' shape with your non-dominant hand, with your palm facing downwards and your fingers extended. Move your dominant hand to form a hook-like shape around the 'L', with your palm facing upwards.
lazy: Hold your arm out in front of you with your palm down, then bring your elbow up so that it is near your shoulder. Lower your forearm back down while keeping your upper arm still.
lean: Tilt head to one side, bringing ear closer to shoulder. Keep gaze directed forward and maintain a straight posture.
learn: Hold hands together in front of you, with fingers interlaced but not touching. Separate the hands, then bring them back together, mimicking the action of opening and closing a book.
legal: Point your dominant index finger upwards, then move it towards yourself while keeping it extended. Your non-dominant hand should be held still, palm facing downwards.
leisure: Hold up an imaginary object or person in front of you, then move it towards you and apart.
leisure activities: Finger-spelling the words L-E-I-S-U-R-E A-C-T-I-V-I-T-I-E-S, or use a sign that represents relaxing or fun activities
lightning: Hold out one arm straight out to the side at shoulder height, with the other arm bent and resting against your thigh. Move both hands rapidly in a zigzag motion, as if conducting electricity.
like: Extend dominant index finger upwards, then bend it down slightly while keeping it extended. Hold this position for a brief moment before returning to the starting position.
little: Hold out one hand with fingers spread apart, palm facing downwards. Move your other hand in an arc shape above the first hand, as if holding something small and delicate.
live: Hold both arms straight out to the sides at shoulder height, palms facing downwards. Bring your arms back towards your body while keeping them extended.
location: Point with dominant index finger to the location you are discussing.
london visit: sign 'L' with one hand and move the other hand around it to indicate visiting, then tap your chest with the first hand to indicate London
look: Extend dominant index finger, then point it in the direction of something you want to draw attention to. Keep your gaze directed at that thing.
looking-up-and-down: Looking up and down to convey the image of a mother looking down at her shorter child.
loud: Hold one hand out in front of you, palm facing downwards. Move your other hand in a sharp, zigzag motion above the first hand, as if conducting sound waves.
lousy: Make a 'dropping' motion with dominant hand, palm facing downwards. Drop imaginary object to the ground and let it fall.
lovely: Hold out one hand, palm up, and move your other hand in a smooth, curving motion above it, as if cradling something delicate.
lucky: Rub dominant thumb over index finger of the same hand in a circular motion. End with the thumb on top of the finger.
m-e-a-s-l-e-s: the fingerspelled name of the sign that Signer A did not understand
m-o-l-a-r: make a circular motion with dominant hand, touch teeth
mad: Rake fingers through hair with both hands, then throw your head back and make a loud noise.
manifold: Mimic spreading or expanding (as in opening a fan)
many sports: Finger-spelling the words M-A-N-Y S-P-O-R-T-S, or use a sign that represents multiple sports
math: signed with the M handshape and ALGEBRA is signed with the A handshape.
medication: pick up an imaginary pill and swallow it, then point to self
medicine: move right hand to the side and left hand to indicate 'with' (similar to ME APPOINTMENT WITH DOCTOR)
medium amount: Make a 'V' shape with both hands, and move them apart
mingle: extend right arm out to the side, with palm up, and move it slightly towards oneself
missing: point to an imaginary gap in the mouth
month: move right hand in a small circle, with fingers spread apart
motor vehicle: represented by the right and left hands as classifiers, passing one another
motorcycle: hold one hand out, palm down, and move it in a circular motion to indicate turning
movement-along-the-horizontal-plane: Movement to the right or left along the horizontal plane typically identifies 'who' is talking.
movement-along-the-vertical-plane: Movement of the head and gazing up and down illustrate the different height or status of a person, and the different height of an object.
my: Point to self with non-dominant hand.
my asl course: raising the eyebrows and tilting the head slightly forward, pausing slightly at the end of the topic, then signing the comment about the course
my room: Point to your own chest, then to the side of your body with your dominant hand.
my schedule empty/full: holds up empty/full hands to represent schedule
my schedule full: make a comment about the topic
my teacher: raising the eyebrows and tilting the head slightly forward, pausing slightly at the end of the topic, then signing the comment about the teacher
negation: Shake your head or make a downward motion with your hand
next: Extend your dominant hand with fingers spread apart, palm facing downwards, and then draw a small line from top to bottom
next month: points to next month (or future time period) using dominant hand
night: Make a circular motion above head with dominant hand.
nine-weeks-ago: use the sign for LAST-WEEK, but with nine fingers extended on the right hand
no: move hands apart and up slightly to indicate 'no'
noisy: the dominant hand makes a loud motion, as if speaking loudly
none: Hold up all five fingers and then drop them to zero, indicating nothing
numbers-15: The numbers 15 are formed with the palm of the hand facing toward the signer's body.
occasionally: hold up three fingers and then drop them down
oh-i-see: wag finger from top to bottom and then nod head
okay: make an 'okay' sign with your dominant hand by touching your thumb and index finger together
old: the dominant hand touches the chin, then moves downward to indicate age
one: Hold one hand up with fingers extended, palm facing forward.
one hour: make a comment about the topic
one-another: hold one's hands together, palms facing each other, and move them apart
one-handed sign: move off to the right side of the body can be made with the right hand, similarly, signs that move to the left side of the body can be made with the left hand
one-week-ago: use the sign for LAST-WEEK, but with a single finger extended on the right hand
open-mouth-wide: Mimic opening your mouth wide by spreading your hands apart in front of your face and moving them up and down.
opposites: Hold both hands out, palms up, then move them apart
our: open hand with the palm facing outward
ours: open hand with the palm facing outward
p-e-n-i-c-i-l-l-i-n: Mimic writing with a pen by moving dominant arm in a smooth motion.
page: Use the dominant hand to point to an imaginary page and then move it slightly backwards
pain: Make a 'cutting' motion with the dominant hand, palm facing down
pain-in-mouth: Touch mouth with index finger and thumb, as if feeling pain.
participate: Hold one hand up, palm facing forward, and move it as if you're participating in an activity
pay: Hold one hand out, palm facing forward, then make a 'giving money' motion with the other hand
people: Hold both hands out, palms down, then move them apart to indicate separation or bring them together to show unity
person or animal: represented by movement in the signing space
pick-on: The right hand moves while the left hand does not.
pick-up: reach toward the addressee and make the sign
picking up: Use the index finger and thumb of your dominant hand to mimic picking up an object
play: Hold hands together in a fist and move them apart
please: move hands apart and up slightly
please appreciate: move hands apart and up slightly for PLEASE, then move dominant hand in a circular motion for APPRECIATE
plumber: make a twisting motion with your dominant hand as if you are tightening a pipe
polite: Hold both hands out, palms down, then move them apart to indicate separation or bring them together to show unity, and make a slight bowing motion with your head
poor: the dominant hand is placed at the bottom of the body and moves upward to indicate lack
poor-thing: This is a directional verb and therefore the middle finger of the hand should be pointing toward the person to whom it is referring.
positions-relating-to-directional-moveme: Directional movements are related to the use of directional verbs, which you learned about in Chapter 6.
practice: hold one's hands together, palms facing each other, and move them apart, then sign PRACTICE
prefer: Move dominant hand from side to side, palm facing forward.
pregnant: Make a 'holding' motion with one hand, palm facing outward, and the other hand on top, as if holding an invisible baby
preposition: Hold both hands out, palms down, then move them apart to indicate separation or bring them together to show unity, and make a slight sweeping motion with your dominant hand
pretend: Use your index finger and thumb of your dominant hand to mimic picking up an object, while moving it back and forth
previous: Move your dominant hand outwards, palm facing forward
pull out: Make a 'pulling' motion with your dominant hand, palm facing outward
pull-out: Hold your cheeks and move them outwards as if pulling something out, or sign TONSILITIS by touching your throat, APPENDECTOMY by touching your right side, or simply TOOTH by signing on top of someone's head.
pull-outhave-to: Hold your cheeks and move them outwards as if pulling something out, or sign TONSILITIS by touching your throat, APPENDECTOMY by touching your right side, then hold up one hand, palm facing outwards, and move it from side to side with a slight bending motion at the wrist.
puny: Hold one hand up, palm down, and make a small, weak motion with your fingers
purchase: Hold both hands out, palms down, then move them apart to indicate separation or bring them together to show unity, and make a 'giving money' motion with the other hand
questionwhy: (1) tilts the head forward and (2) raises the eyebrows
quitecalm: the non-dominant hand is placed on top of the head, with the dominant hand on the lap
race: Run in place, pumping your arms as if you're running
rage: Clutch your chest or stomach with both hands, then move them outward and downward
rainbow: Draw a semi-circle shape with one hand, palm facing down
real time reporting: Hold up your watch, then point to it, indicating 'real-time'
real-time reporter: Hold a notebook and pen up, then type quickly with an imaginary stenographic machine.
really: Tap the opposite shoulder with your dominant index finger, keeping the rest of the fingers curled in
recently: Place your dominant index finger on your nose, and then draw a small circle around it, moving it outward and upward
red: Draw a vertical line with one hand, palm down, and make an arrow shape pointing downward
regret: Clutch your chest or stomach with both hands, then move them outward and downward
reject: Hold one hand out, palm facing forward, then make a 'pushing away' motion with the other hand
relationships: Draw a circle with both hands, palms down, to show connection or link between two people
relatives: Point to yourself, another person, an object, or a place, depending on who is being referred to as a relative
relax: Let your arms drop and move them outward, palms up, indicating 'relaxation'
relay service: Hold out two hands palms up, then bring them together and apart in a 'link' motion.
repeat: Mimic writing in the air with one hand, palm facing down, repeating the motion
request: Hold one hand out, palm facing forward, then make a 'asking' gesture with the other hand, moving it upward and slightly to the right
respect: Bow your head slightly, as if showing respect or deference
rest: Lie down on an imaginary surface with both hands behind you, palms up
rest must: Make a resting motion with your body, followed by pointing to someone else, indicating they must rest
restaurant: Draw a small square shape with one hand, palm facing down, then make a 'eating' motion
ride-on: the hand moves forward to represent going someplace
right-fly-back: move from the outside of the signing space toward the inside
room number 4: hold up four fingers on the dominant hand, with palm facing upwards
root sign: Hold one hand up, palm facing forward, and move it in a circular motion, as if drawing a root or stem
rot: Twist your hands as if they're rotting, palms down
rotten: Mimic rotting by moving your hands apart and bringing them together in a slow, downward motion.
round: Make an 'O' shape with both hands, with fingers spread apart
rude: Cover your mouth with both hands, then shake them back and forth
ruin: Sign ROOTTEN then move your hands from side to side with a slight bending motion at the wrist.
ruined: Clutch your chest or stomach with both hands, then move them outward and downward
same-here: make a similar sign to ME FEEL GOOD, but with both hands held at chest level
satisfied: A sign made by rubbing the chest or shoulder with the dominant hand, indicating satisfaction or contentment
schedule: Hold up one or both hands, palms facing outwards, and move them in a circular motion as if turning pages in a book.
school: Move the dominant hand to your forehead and then down to your nose, as if tracing the shape of a school building.
science: Use the dominant hand to draw an imaginary triangle in the air with your index finger, palm facing upwards.
scold: A sharp, downward hand movement with the fingers spread apart.
seattle: Move the dominant hand from your chin to your shoulder, then up towards the sky, and finally bring it back down to your chin.
secretary: Use both hands to mimic holding a pen or pencil and writing on an imaginary notepad.
see: Make a 'window' shape with your dominant hand by separating your thumb and index finger, and then move it as if looking through a window.
self: Point to yourself with the dominant hand while making a fist with your other hand.
show: Sign SHOW moving the hands from the addressee to yourself;
shy: While signing SHY a person could (1) raise the right shoulder and (2) tilt the head slightly toward the same shoulder
sick: Make a circular motion above head with dominant hand.
sign: Draw an imaginary sign language alphabet in the air with your dominant hand, starting from the top and moving downwards.
sign different: extend right hand, palm up, and move it towards oneself, with a difference in finger movement
silent: Move the dominant hand across your lips as if wiping away saliva or smudging lipstick.
silly: Twirl your index finger around the tip of your thumb, as if you're blowing a bubble.
simple: Make a 'flat' shape with your dominant hand by spreading out your fingers and palm.
sing: Move your arms up and down in a circular motion, as if conducting an orchestra.
sister: Point to your cheek or nose with the dominant hand while making a fist with your other hand.
sit: Place your seat on the chair
sit-down: Lower yourself into a seated position, mimicking sitting down in a chair.
skill: Draw an imaginary triangle in the air with your index finger, palm facing upwards.
skilled: Use both hands to mimic holding a tool or object and moving it as if working on something.
skilledadept: the dominant hand is placed in front of the chest, with fingers spread apart
skinny: Hold up one hand and move it from side to side, as if measuring something with a ruler.
sleepy: Rub your eyes with the dominant hand while making a sleepy face.
small amount: Move your non-dominant hand from left to right across the dominant hand
small container-like objects: Cup your non-dominant hand, and move it to the location of the object
smoke: Mimic holding a cigarette or cigar and taking a puff.
smoke never: make a smoking gesture with the hand (holding an imaginary cigarette)
sneeze: Cover your nose and mouth with the dominant hand and pretend to sneeze.
snow: Move both hands up and down, as if throwing snowballs.
socialization: Deaf people feel a strong need to socialize with each other, they crave the social contact that is absent from their homelife, school, and workplace when no one else signs in these places.
socialize: Move both arms in a circular motion, as if hugging someone.
socializeacquaint: extend right hand, palm up, and move it towards oneself
something: Point to an imaginary object or gesture with the dominant hand while making a questioning expression.
sometimes: Mimic holding a calendar or planner and flipping through its pages.
song: Move your arms up and down in a circular motion, as if conducting an orchestra.
soon: Look at an imaginary clock or watch on your wrist and move it forward.
sore: Similar to PAIN, touch the affected area, but more pronounced and slower.
sorry: Cover your mouth with the dominant hand while making a sorry face.
speak: Mimic holding a microphone or speaking into an imaginary receiver.
speech: Mimic speaking into an imaginary receiver while holding up one hand to your ear.
spell: Draw each letter of the word in the air with your index finger, starting from the top and moving downwards.
spoil: Mimic holding something precious and dropping it, as if spoiling it.
sport: Finger-spelling the word S-P-O-R-T, or use a sports-related sign such as waving an imaginary tennis racket
sports: Move both arms in a circular motion, as if playing a sport like tennis or basketball.
startled: Jump backwards quickly, as if startled by something.
starved: Mimic eating or drinking, as if you're starving.
steak: Hold up one hand and mimic holding a steak while slicing it with an imaginary knife.
steal: Mimic taking something from someone else's hands while making a sly face.
stomachache: Rub your stomach with the dominant hand while making a painful expression.
stop: Hold up one hand, palm facing outwards, and move it as if stopping something.
story: Mimic telling a story by moving both hands in a circular motion, as if holding a pen or pencil.
strange: Move your head from side to side, as if confused or puzzled.
strength: Hold up one hand and mimic lifting something heavy with the other hand.
strong: Mimic pushing against an invisible force with both hands.
student: Point to your head or forehead with the dominant hand while making a studying expression.
students: the hands move to the side (indicates plural form of TEACHER)
study: Mimic holding a book or notebook and moving it as if studying.
stupid: Make a foolish face and mimic dropping something, as if you're stupid.
summer: Move both arms up and down, as if enjoying the warm weather.
summon: Hold up one hand and move it towards an imaginary object or person.
sun: Mimic holding an invisible sun with your dominant hand while smiling.
sunday: Point to the sky or heaven with the dominant hand while making a peaceful expression.
super: Hold up both hands, palms facing outwards, and move them as if lifting something heavy.
support: Hold up one hand, palm facing downwards, and move it as if supporting something.
suppose: Mimic holding an imaginary question mark in front of you with your dominant hand.
supposeif: two hands are used, one for SUPPOSE and the other for IF; SUPPOSE is on the head, palm facing outwards, and IF is on the top of the head, fingers spread apart
sure: Nod your head or make a confident expression while moving the dominant hand in a 'yes' motion.
surgery: Mimic operating on someone or something with an imaginary scalpel.
surprised: Jump backwards quickly, as if surprised by something.
sweden: Move the dominant hand from your chin to your shoulder, then up towards the sky, and finally bring it back down to your chin.
swift: Move both arms up and down rapidly, as if swimming through water.
swim: Mimic moving your arms like a fish while making a splashing sound.
switzerland go-to: sign 'S' with one hand and point to Switzerland with the other
synonyms: Mimic holding an imaginary dictionary or encyclopedia with both hands.
tactile signal: Hold out flat palm and make a vibrating motion by wiggling fingers.
take: Mimic taking something from an imaginary source, such as a cup of coffee or a book.
take-up science: Hold up both hands with palms facing each other and fingers spread apart, then move them upwards as if lifting something.
tale: Mimic telling a story by moving both hands in a circular motion, as if holding a pen or pencil.
tall: Hold up one hand and mimic measuring something with a ruler while looking upwards.
teach: Mimic holding a chalkboard or whiteboard marker and writing on an invisible board.
teacher: Point to your head or forehead with the dominant hand while making a teaching expression.
tease: sign 'tease' with your dominant hand, palm facing outwards, and move it in a teasing motion towards the person
technical: Hold up one hand, palm facing outwards, and move it as if handling technical equipment.
technology: Mimic holding a smartphone or computer and moving it as if using technology.
temporary: Hold up both hands, palms facing inwards, and move them as if covering something temporary.
tennis: Move both arms in a circular motion, as if playing tennis.
terrific: Hold up one hand and mimic lifting something heavy with the other hand while smiling.
thank: Make a 'thank you' expression and move the dominant hand as if receiving gratitude.
theater: Mimic holding an imaginary script or playbill and moving it as if acting in a theater.
their: open hand with the palm facing outward
them: Point to someone else, such as a friend or family member, with the dominant hand while making a 'them' expression.
then: Hold up one hand and mimic looking at an imaginary clock or watch on your wrist.
there: Point to an imaginary location with the dominant hand while making a 'there' expression.
thermometer: Mimic holding an imaginary thermometer and taking its temperature.
think: Rub your chin or forehead with the dominant hand while making a thinking expression.
this week: taps index finger on chin, then points to the week (or month) being discussed
thoughts: Draw an imaginary triangle in the air with your index finger, palm facing upwards.
three-weeks: hold up three fingers on one hand and move it forward, keeping the other hand still
thrilled: Make a happy face and mimic jumping up and down, as if thrilled about something.
thunder: Mimic holding an imaginary storm cloud or thunderbolt and moving it as if creating thunder.
thursday: Point to the sky or heaven with the dominant hand while making a peaceful expression.
tickets: Hold up one hand and mimic holding tickets, such as concert or movie tickets.
time: Mimic looking at an imaginary clock or watch on your wrist and moving it forward.
time 3: three fingers are extended from the dominant hand, palm facing downwards
timid: Make a shy face and mimic hiding behind an imaginary object, such as a curtain or door.
tiny: Hold up one hand and mimic holding something small with the other hand.
tired: Rub your eyes with the dominant hand while making a tired expression.
tiresome: Mimic falling asleep or yawning, as if you're tired of something.
title: Mimic holding an imaginary book or document and moving it as if reading its title.
today: Look at an imaginary calendar or planner on your wrist and move it forward.
today/yesterday: The body and the signing space combine to indicate time in ASL. The body itself represents the present time or present tense. That's why the signs TODAY and NOW move in a vertical plane close to the body. To indicate the future, signs move to the front of the body (e.g., TOMORROW). To indicate the past tense, signs move toward the back of the body.
tomorrow: Hold up one hand and mimic looking at an imaginary clock or watch on your wrist, pointing to the future.
tonight: Mimic holding an imaginary schedule or calendar and moving it as if planning for tonight.
tooth: Mimic brushing your teeth with an imaginary toothbrush while making a healthy expression.
topic/comment: uses a nonmanual signal (e.g. head orientation) to indicate topic/comment structure
toronto: Move the dominant hand from your chin to your shoulder, then up towards the sky, and finally bring it back down to your chin.
touch: Mimic touching something or someone with an imaginary finger.
tour: Mimic holding an imaginary map or guidebook and moving it as if leading a tour.
train: Mimic riding on an invisible train, such as sitting in a passenger car or standing at the platform.
train get-on: point to a train and mimic getting on by moving your arm up
tranquil: Make a peaceful face and mimic floating on water, as if you're tranquil about something.
transfer: Hold up one hand, palm facing outwards, and move it as if transferring something from one place to another.
travel: Mimic holding an imaginary suitcase or backpack and moving it as if traveling somewhere.
trip: Mimic taking a trip by mimicking driving a car or flying on an airplane.
trivial: Make a dismissive face and mimic throwing something away, as if it's trivial.
tty: Use the dominant hand and make a 'T' shape with your thumb and index finger.
turning-shoulders-and-head-to-the-right-: Turning the shoulders and head to the right or left.
twisting the hand: twist counterclockwise for signs made with the right hand, and clockwise for signs made with the left hand
two: use two hands and fingers to represent the number
two-weeks-ago: use the sign for LAST-WEEK, but with two fingers extended on the right hand
uncle: Tap the side of the non-dominant fist with the tip of the index finger
understand: Hold hands up in front of the chest, palms facing outwards and fingers spread wide.
united-states: make a flat 'O' shape with thumb and index finger, while extending arm out to the side
united-states canada: make a flat 'O' shape with thumb and index finger for each country, while extending arm out to the side
until: Finger-spelled: U-N-T-I-L-L (each letter formed by signing the corresponding handshape)
upset: Make a fist with one hand, and with the other hand make a 'C' shape with your thumb and index finger near the fist.
use: Hold out an open hand, palm facing upwards.
vacant: Hold hands up in front of the chest, palms facing outwards and fingers spread wide (as if holding something).
valid: Make a fist with one hand, and with the other hand make a 'C' shape with your thumb and index finger near the fist.
valuable: Place your dominant hand on top of your non-dominant hand, palm facing upwards.
video called: the dominant hand is on the forehead, palm facing upwards, and the non-dominant hand points to something
videophone: Hold a camera up to the ear, then point at a screen or monitor.
volleyball: Mimic hitting a ball with a paddle (fist), then extend arm as if tossing the ball away
w-g-d: make a flat 'O' shape with thumb and index finger, while extending arm out to the side ( classifier for World Games for the Deaf )
wait: Hold up a flat palm facing outwards.
wait please: Make a waiting motion with your hand, palm facing outwards, and then point to yourself or someone else
waive: Wave goodbye with your dominant hand.
watch: Move your dominant hand as if turning on a TV or camera, starting at the shoulder and moving down to the wrist.
watch-go-by: fingertips move from one side to the other side to indicate that the eyes moved as something went by
wealthy: Make an 'L' shape by bending the middle finger downward
wednesday: Hold up all five fingers on one hand, then point to each day of the week in order.
week: Hold up all five fingers on one hand
what: Raise an eyebrow and ask with dominant hand
what kind: Raise an eyebrow and ask with dominant hand (WHAT)
when: Point to a spot somewhere on your body (representing time)
where: Hold up both hands, palms facing each other, and move them apart as if opening doors.
why: Shake head from side to side
with: Move the dominant hand towards the non-dominant hand
year: Hold up all five fingers on one hand, then point forward
you: Point to self or another person with non-dominant hand.
your: pointing to the person or thing being asked about, followed by a series of descriptive terms
your room: Hold the dominant hand in front of you, palm down. Move the hand toward yourself while keeping the forearm still.
yours: open hand with the palm facing outward
yout: Point to self or another person with non-dominant hand.
yucky: Open '5' handshape moves away from the chin while the face shows a disgusted expression.
ride: Dominant 'U' handshape hooks over the thumb of the non-dominant 'C' handshape, moving forward together.
blow: Hold a loose 'O' handshape near mouth and blow air out, moving hand forward.
beside: Both flat hands pointing forward side-by-side, dominant hand moves further out to the side.
minemy: Place a flat hand firmly against the center of your chest.
mitten: Slide one flat hand over the other flat hand, keeping fingers together, as if pulling on a mitten.
yourself: Push an 'A' handshape with thumb pointing up forward toward the person you are addressing.
cowboy: Mimic holding reins with both fists, moving them back and forth, or mimic drawing two pistols.
haveto: An 'X' handshape moves sharply downward once.
another: Closed fist with thumb extended upward ('A' or '10' handshape), flip the hand over in an arc to point to the side.
owie: Both index fingers point at each other and twist in opposite directions near the site of the pain.
jeans: Both hands form 'A' handshapes and rub up and down the sides of the upper thighs/hips.
